title: Covalent bonding
summary: 9701 AS covalent and dative covalent bonding — dot-and-cross diagrams, bond polarity, and molecular examples with PhET Build a Molecule.
learningObjectives: Describe covalent bonding as shared electron pairs.
Draw dot-and-cross diagrams for simple molecules and ions.
Explain dative covalent (coordinate) bonding with examples (NH₄⁺, Al₂Cl₆).
Relate bond polarity to electronegativity differences.

simpleExplanation.title: Shared electrons
simpleExplanation.summary: Covalent bonds form when atoms share electron pairs. In a dative bond, both electrons in the pair come from one atom — common in ammonium and expanded octets.
steps: Single bond = one shared pair; double = two pairs; triple = three pairs.
Draw dots/crosses to show which atom donated which electrons.
Dative bond: lone pair from donor atom → empty orbital on acceptor (e.g. H⁺).
Polar covalent: unequal sharing — δ+ and δ− from electronegativity difference.

sections.content: **Covalent bonding** (3.4) connects to **shapes** (3.5) and **intermolecular forces** (3.6). Cambridge tests **NH₄⁺**, **H₃O⁺**, and **Al₂Cl₆** as dative bond examples.

sections.question: Draw the formation of NH₄⁺ from NH₃ and H⁺, showing the dative bond.
sections.solution:
NH₃ has a lone pair on N.

H⁺ has empty 1s orbital.

N donates lone pair to H⁺ → **dative covalent bond**.

All four N–H bonds in NH₄⁺ are equivalent once formed.

sections.content: Use an **arrow** from lone pair to H⁺ when showing dative bond formation. After formation, draw all bonds as ordinary lines in the ion.
